sentences.text: The one that I know was-- one Kapo was Kojak.
That was a very good one.
You know, very helpful, you know the guy that, in the beginning, you know.
Because if he wasn't nice enough, you know, you couldn't survive that either.
But the overall Kapo of the Crematorium II, where I was working, was by the name of Kaminski.
He was very tough, but also, inside, I think, of this man was something very, very good in it.
And he knew about the-- we used to make a-- would like to make a revolution there, an uprising.
OK.
Well, Kaminski was one-- the overall Kapo.
He was tough with us, but I think, his heart, and he was a very nice, nice man.
Both Polish, Jewish men, yeah.
I don't know, just [LAUGHS] we try.
We-- we had an upstairs on the crematorium.
Upstairs were nice, you know, bunkers to sleep.
Wasn't bad for us, you know.
We-- we had good clothes, you know.
We-- our clothes weren't the one with the-- like the camp.
We would take a jacket from the people that were coming, and we had a jacket and a shirt and--
Because every six months, the Sonderkommando was always killed, gassed, and new people come in.
This is how we came in.
From-- from people that were there.
They know that, every six months, there were a selection, a selection going on.
You know, always, always, there's a replenishing.
You know?
So we knew that it was impossible.
We knew exactly, from A to Z, how the final solution of the Jewish people was meant.
And I'm going to tell you now that, as far as I know, we are only three survivors today in-- two in the United States.
I think four.
Two in the United States, one in Rome, and one in-- I think, in Tel Aviv.
And when we are gone, you know, I don't think anybody-- anybody can tell that.
I know that somebody was there, and that's what happened.
But I-- we are only eyewitness of the final solution.
That's why it's very important.
And that's why I went-- you know, a few weeks ago, I just came back from Poland, you know-- with the young people that are hungry of knowledge what was going on for the Holocaust.
Well, they-- the SS told us to search every-- everything that was coming in.
So we had the facility to take anything and put it aside.
You know, also, one of our members, a Greek guy by the name of Cohen, you know, that used to extract the gold teeth.

And, you know, they-- when-- when it happened that we know we are not going to survive, and we wanted to do something-- an uprising, a revolution, you know-- that was some things we were sending-- the partisans were about 30 miles away from us.
They were not too cooperative with us.
But just we were sending money to them, and they were sending knives, dynamite to be able to-- to do something one day.
And that's what happened.
Through parties.
You know, there's always means. you know, people-- with money, you can do anything.
Well, the-- the Germans were every day taking them away, you know.
You put it in a-- in a can, whatever it was, and they will take them.
As a matter of fact, I was listening the other day that, in Switzerland, all this-- I don't know if you see last week-- all that gold belongs to the Jewish people.
Well, they had money, dollars.
A lot of people come with dollars.
They had diamonds.
Any-- you know, most of their possessions, they could put it in places, you know, that nobody would see it.
You know, that--
Different places.
Especially when they were-- after-- after they were dead.
A lot of things were found, you know, in ears, in-- I can't even mention.
The-- if we were around there, we would remove it.
But we couldn't do nothing with it, because there's no place to do it, you know.
They were going to the Germans.
Always you talk about the old things, about the nice things, to be free.
To be able to do that, to be with your family.
You know, we were young people.
We were just in the stage of our lives, you know, that you need everything, you know.
To be able to survive, and to have a nice life.
That didn't happen.
Most of the time, we were doing that.
And that took most of our time, because we were working, especially the time I was there.
There were a lot of work to be done.
You know, it never stopped.
I was 22.
We were together with my brother, and the two brothers of Venezia, and Danny, and Marcel Nadjary that-- he survived.
He was the one that, I think, in 1980, they found a bottle in the crematoriums with a few notes of his.
He used to keep notes.
He didn't tell anyone.
He has a book in Greek that came out.
Oh, sure, sure.
We cared [INAUDIBLE].
Life is too precious, you know.
And we were too young to die.
That's why we decide to make an uprising.
And the uprising was initiated by-- there were 19 Russians, all from the army.
Officers.
And was one Greek captain by the name-- by the name of Baruch.
That they put together the uprising.
And actually, they put a date on the uprising, and the date that couldn't be kept was October 7, 1944.
And the date couldn't be kept because, at that time, there were a lot of-- it happens that that day, a lot of Polish people came to the camp.
I don't know why.
I don't know if I had gypsies, but we knew that there were gypsies there, yeah.
They were killing a lot of Polish, too, you know?
But, you know, 99.9% were the Jewish people.
Anyone that has a number is Jewish.
The people that were not Jews, they didn't have no number.
I didn't know.
I didn't know, but I know that Mengele was doing experiments, you know, in Block 10, in Auschwitz.
I think there is a lady here, living here-- I don't know if you-- you interview her?
No?
But that's the name-- by the name of [?
Taboch ?] that I think was on that block.
Yeah, always I work in Crematorium II.
Yeah, there were one, two, three, four across.
Across.
Not too far away.
No.
You could see everything that was happening.
No, but you know, always when there were-- if it wasn't enough of a transport, there always was 10, 20, 30 people.
We-- they were coming, we had to take them by the ear, and wait in there, and behind the German will kill them.
Go down and clean up the-- and going to the ovens.
If there were-- if there no were-- when there were 1,500 or 2,000, then they put it in the gas chambers, but for a small amount, you know, they used to shoot it by individual.
Done in front of us, in the crematorium.
You have to pick it up by the ear.
Bring them that and keep them by the ear, and the SS would be behind, shooting them.
You know, they would die immediately.
Would go down, and somebody would pick it up, and then we had to clean up the blood.
Things that I-- I don't know, I just-- it's very hard to say it.
I don't know, to stand up.
I don't-- to not to move.
SS.
There were, yeah.
I don't remember their names.
But there-- one was a Holland guy, from Holland, SS.
He wasn't doing that.
But there was another one.
I don't remember his name.
He was a tall, red-faced man that-- he was doing most of the shootings.
Tall, very tall.
They were OK with us, you know.
We didn't-- we never-- we didn't have too much to do with them, really.
They used to come, you know, after there were-- there were-- before we open up the gas chamber, they used to come frequently from Berlin.
I don't know their names.
They must be people from Berlin seeing how the Jewish people were dying.
Most of the time, they were happy people.
Yes.
Yes.
There were plenty of SS women, you know, around the crematorium there, you know?
Especially on the-- when the bunkers were in use, you know.
There were a lot of women around there.
Well, when there weren't enough transports, that the gas chambers couldn't hold it, you know, then they had to, you know-- after the gas chamber, the crematoriums couldn't hold thousands of people.
You know, there were-- at one point, there were coming there, what, 5,000 6,000, 7,000, 10,000 a day.
Yeah.
Crematorium people would put the bodies.
It takes-- it takes hours, you know, for 2,000 people to be burned.
Yes.
Oh, sure.
Probably there were.
I don't-- I don't remember.
Well, we were told by-- by the people that put the uprising together that there will be an uprising, because we had to take part of it.
Not only Crematorium II, but the III and IV together.
Yeah, yeah, we were in contact.
But the-- what I remember is that the-- the-- we couldn't keep that October 7 date, because of that.
So then, Kominski was killed, you know.
And then, the next-- after a few days, they-- they selected 200 from the crematorium-- from all the crematorium, 200 people-- and they-- they gassed them, and killed.
The SS selects.
You know, always they were with names.
They come with the numbers, you know, and they selected.
And they-- they were-- time, you know, at that point, I-- if I remember correctly, it's just-- there were, in October, there were-- the transports were easing up.
There weren't enough transports.
But there were the Polish people, at that time in the Crematorium II, didn't want us to do it, because this would be for no avail.
So we didn't did nothing.
So the hundred of us were taken to a room, and then Commandant Kramer came in, and registered all our numbers.

Well, there were-- I don't know exactly, but the 19-- the 19 Russians, and-- and the Greek guy, you know, they got together, and with the help of sending gold, or sending money there, we used to get to all this from, I think, a manufacturer around-- around there called Union.
U-N- I- O-N.
We were told to-- to get dressed well, you know?
That-- that's the only thing.
We-- we supposed-- we supposed to-- to cut the tires of anything that was from the SS.
And also, if we could kill the SS.
There was one SS in Crematorium II.
I don't remember.
Could you [INAUDIBLE]?
What happened is that we went upstairs in our rooms to get better view of what was happening.
And--
We were watching, but we couldn't see anything.
Also, you know, Crematorium I was-- was calling us.
So I don't remember exactly what happened, you know?
Well, they see-- when they see the fires, you know, they thought that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Crematorium I, they thought that the Germans were killing all of us.
So what they did, you know, they-- one of the Greeks in Crematorium I says, let's go.
Let's start the uprising.
And-- but-- and they had also the dynamite, and they didn't put the dynamite.
So what happened is the Germans, the SS came in, and they kill everybody in Crematorium I.
And what happened also is that, you know, the next day, they brought all the corpses of the Crematorium I, and we had to put them, you know, in the ovens.
Yeah.
Nothing, just burn them.
Yeah, we were surrounded by SS.
And-- and also, you know, in-- in Crematorium-- Crematorium III, you know?
Also that was also the-- they went and they-- this SS came in, and they start shooting, on site, everybody.
No, they start shooting.
I-- we don't know what-- how it happens.
That's why didn't it succeed, the revolution.
We were-- we weren't coordinated enough to be able to do it.
And then, as I remember, they were telling me that the-- the partisans, they weren't cooperative enough for us, the Jews, to do the job.
Yeah, Kramer.
Nothing.
They just-- just-- you know, I thought that we were going to get killed.
But--
Nothing.
Just, we didn't do anything.
Then-- then that happened in Crematorium I, you know, and then everybody was killed.
But after that, what was happening is that the they-- they-- the transports stopped.
[INAUDIBLE] You know, they came from-- they selected another 200.
And then they came from Berlin.
Came-- you know, they-- they came in from Berlin and they say to stop-- stop killing.
Stop killing, and the transports stopped.
This is-- this is how everything started.
Now when we were-- when that happens, we were-- the Sonderkommando, whatever were left of us-- I think 200 of us were left-- we were just cleaning the burned Crematorium III.
The burned crematorium, yeah.
We were cleaning it out.
You know, it was all burned up.
No, nothing, nothing.
Because most-- most of the people, a lot of the people of the Crematorium I and III, you know, they start going in the fields.
This is where the SS just kill everybody.
We were just cleaning the Crematorium I-- I and-- no, Crematorium III.
And-- and then, we-- on January 15, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the Germans were uneasy because the Russians were advancing from Krakow to Auschwitz.
Kojak was one of the Kapos of Crematorium II.
So we were only left, now, 100 of us, and-- and 26 Greeks.
And January-- January 15, when the Russians were coming, the-- on January 18, the Birkenau-- Auschwitz and Birkenau-- the Birkenau actually was evacuated.
And we were on that Block 13.
And the Germans were mixed up.
They didn't know what they were doing, so we came out of that Block 13, and we got together with all the camp, the thousands of people, and start marching.
They put us to march from Birkenau to Auschwitz.
That took quite a few hours, about three miles, two and a half miles.
I don't know who I was with.
I was 20-20 marching.
I was alone. 20-20 marching.
I think my brother and Danny was together, but I was with other people, 20-20 marching.
We march to Auschwitz, and from there, after stopping a few hours, we start walking for days.
Two, three days walking.
And I remember then very perfectly was the-- the 18th of January, 1945, and outside was 23 below zero.
And the only thing that I can tell you today is that I survived by closing my eyes and saying, how beautiful Athens with the sunshine.
And repeating that, many times, I start sweating.
Believe me, I am telling you now.
I had a blanket, and I threw it out.
And we marched, and we marched for days until we arrive to a railroad station, where they put us in open wagons, there-- 150 in each one.
And we-- after a day or so, we arrive at Mauthausen.
Marching thousands of people.
And whoever couldn't march, the-- the army, the SS, was behind us.
And they would kill anyone that they couldn't make it.
So we arrive much, much less in our destination of Mauthausen.
No, nothing.
When we left Auschwitz, we had, I think, 150 grams of bread and some margarine.
That's the only thing.
I remember that I was walking in Linz, and was raining.
Was raining, and I-- I got down, and I put mud in my-- I was eating mud.
Mud.
OK.
